records.content:
There are a number of ways to install python locally on your computer. We recommend using the 

miniforge distribution.

Head to the linked page and download the installer suitable for your OS. Once the installer is downloaded, follow the installation instruction and install the miniforge distribution. Once miniforge is installed, start a terminal (command prompt in Windows, Terminal in MacOS, etc.) with the base mamba environment activated (in Windows there is a separate icon for that, in Mac your Terminal should be configured to activate the base environment automatically). A mamba enabled terminal with the base environment activated should have a prompt that started with the text (base).

Figure: A mamba enabled terminal.

The base environment has a very limited number of third-party packages installed. So our next step is to install the third-party packages that are commonly used in scientific computing (numpy, scipy, pandas, matplotlib, etc.). While we can install these packages in the base environment, we recommend doing so in a new environment. In this appendix we’ll call this new environment “osym”, but you should feel free to change that name in the subsequent code.

The easiest way to create a new environment with the desired packages installed is to use a requirements.txt file. You can find an example of the requirements.txt file 

here.

Download the requirements.txt file to your local computer, then, change directory to the location of the requirements file, and execute the following command:    mamba create -n osym --file requirements.txt

(change the name osym to whatever way you want to name your environment)

Once the above command is entered, mamba should get to work and figure out what packages you need to install (some of the modules included in requirement.txt have prerequisites that are not listed, and mamba will take care of installing those too). Confirm that the installation plan is correct, and mamba will create the environment and install the packages in it.

Once mamba is finished, follow the instruction it provides to activate the osym environment, namely:    mamba activate osym

When the osym environment is activated, the prompt of the terminal should start with the text (osym).

Figure: A terminal with the “osym” environment activated.

Instead of binder, in a local installation you will view your Jupyter notebooks using JupyterLab. To view your notebook in JupyterLab, first open a mamba-enabled terminal, then activate the osym environment (via mamba activate osym) and change directory (via cd) to the folder containing your notebook in the terminal. Next, in the same terminal, run:    jupyter lab

Your browser should automatically open with a JupyterLab page (which has similar interface as in binder) that shows your notebook. You can now work on the notebook like you did on binder.

Occasionally, you may find the need to install additional third-party packages. As a concrete example, let’s suppose I need the 

PyMC package to perform Markov chain Monte Carlo.

To install pymc, open a mamba-enabled terminal and activate the environment for which you want to add the new package. Now, if your package is available on conda-forge (which you can check via 

this page), it is preferable to use mamba to install the package. Using our example, it turns out that pymc is available on conda-forge, so to install I will need to execute:    mamba install pymc

Mamba will then figure out what packages need to be installed, and once you confirm the changes the package will be installed to your environment and available for all future use.

If your package is not available on conda-forge it is likely distributed via pypi. Such packages can be installed using pip. For example, if I want to install PyMC via pip, I will have to run:    pip install -U pymc

(Note that if your package is available on conda-forge and you’ve already run the mamba install line then you do not need to perform pip install. In any case, only one of the two lines are needed).

As with mamba install, the new package is now permanently a part of your python environment and you all you need to do to use it in a notebook is to import the package.

To install and use other packages, just replace pymc in the above lines with the name of the package you need.

hierarchy.lvl1: Basic jupyter notebook and python operations
Basic jupyter notebook and python operations
Basic jupyter notebook and python operations
Basic jupyter notebook and python operations

records.content:
A Jupyter notebook is comprised of cells

There are two main type of cells: markdown (for rich-text display) and code (for executing python code)

There are two modes of operation when working with a Jupyter notebook: the command mode and the edit mode. The former is for cell-level operations while the latter is for editing the content within a cell.

To run the codes in the current cell, press Shift + Enter in either edit mode or command mode. You can also use the play button (⯈) on the top of the main panel to execute the current cell.

Shift + Enter can also be used to render the current markdown cell, if it is not already rendered.

Some useful operations in command mode:

m converts the current cell into a markdown cell

y converts the current cell into a code cell

a inserts a cell above the current cell

b inserts a cell below the current cell

c copies the content of the current cell, and v pastes it

d + d deletes the current cell

z undoes the previous action

Some useful markdown formatting:

To create a list, start the line with a +, followed by a space

To set a block of texts in boldface, surround the block with a pair of **

To italicize a block of texts, surround the block with a pair of _

To set a block of text in monospace, surround the block with a pair of `

To create headings, start the line with a sequence of #, followed by a space (the more # the lower the heading in the hierarchy)

Important: The python kernel remembers the order of code execution but not the order of code presentation!

records.content:
Consider a population that follows the logistic growth model:p_{n+1} = p_{n} + r p_{n} \left( 1 - \frac{p_{n}}{K} \right)

Write a function that takes r, K, p_{1}, and N as input, and produce list of the size of the population from the first (given) to the N-th generation

To make plots in python we need a third-party package. The most common package for such purpose is matplotlib

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt



In addition, we will use numpy to generate theoretical data and use pandas to load observed data:

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

Let’s generate arrays that represent the x and y coordiantes of the function y = \sqrt{x}:

x_array = np.linspace(0, 1, 100)
y_array = np.sqrt(x_array)



x_array



y_array



Note 1: np.linspace() creates a 1D array of evenly spaced points between two endpoints specified. For more information about creating various 1D array, consult numpy’s 

guide on array creation.

Note 2: In general, a numpy function (e.g., np.sqrt() in this case) applies its mapping to each element of the input array. For more examples of numpy functions, see the numpy 

mathematical functions page.

Let’s make a line plot of the square root function.

matplotlib provides two plotting interfaces: one using the global plt object and the other using instances of Figure and Axes objects.

The plt interface is less verbose, but the local objects interface is more feature-complete.

# using the global `plt` interface
plt.plot(x_array, y_array)
plt.show()



# using the local object interface
fig = plt.figure()
ax = fig.add_subplot()
ax.plot(x_array, y_array)
plt.show(fig)

Figure: The correpondence between plot elements and their object-oriented representations in matplotlib. Adopted from matplotlib 

showcase.

Completing our previous plot by adding plot elements and specifying styles:

fig = plt.figure()
ax = fig.add_subplot()

ax.plot(x_array, y_array, lw=2, ls="--", c="tab:purple")

ax.set_xlim([0, 1])
ax.set_ylim([0, 1])

ax.grid(ls=":")

ax.set_xticks(np.arange(0, 1.05, 0.25))
ax.set_yticks(np.arange(0, 1.05, 0.25))

ax.set_xticks(np.arange(0, 1.01, 0.05), minor=True)
ax.set_yticks(np.arange(0, 1.01, 0.05), minor=True)
ax.tick_params("both", labelsize=11)

ax.set_title("The square root function", fontsize=14)
ax.set_xlabel("x-coordinate", fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylabel("y-coordinate", fontsize=12)

plt.show(fig)



For more information about the various plot elements, refer to the matplotlib 

“Introduction to Axes” page and the resources therein.

# scatter plot

x_array = np.random.normal(0, 1, 100)
y_array = 0.2 * x_array + 0.8 * np.random.normal(0, 1, 100)

fig = plt.figure()
ax = fig.add_subplot()

ax.scatter(x_array, y_array, marker=".")

plt.show(fig)



# error bar plot

x_data = np.arange(0, 5.1, 0.5)
y_data = np.sqrt(x_data)
y_err = np.array([
    [0.4, 0.3, 0.3, 0.2, 0.4, 0.4, 0.3, 0.2, 0.2, 0.3, 0.3], # lower
    [0.1, 0.2, 0.2, 0.1, 0.2, 0.2, 0.1, 0.1, 0.2, 0.2, 0.2] # Upper
])
x_err = 0.1

fig = plt.figure()
ax = fig.add_subplot()

plt.errorbar(
    x_data, y_data, yerr = y_err, xerr = x_err, 
    color="blue", marker="o", lw=1, ls="--", 
    ecolor="black", capsize=3.0
)

plt.show(fig)





# bar plot with error bars
# different color for each bar

x_data = np.arange(0, 5)
y_data = np.array([6, 5, 9, 4, 7])

y_err = np.array([0.7, 0.4, 0.3, 0.5, 0.2])

fig = plt.figure()
ax = fig.add_subplot()

ax.bar(
    x_data, y_data, align="center", width=0.8,
    tick_label = ["apple", "banana", "lemon", "grape", "orange"],
    color=["tab:blue", "tab:orange", "tab:green", "tab:red", "tab:purple"],
    yerr = y_err, ecolor="black", capsize=5
)

plt.show(fig)





# 2D false color plot

X = np.linspace(-2, 2, 201)
Y = np.linspace(-1, 1, 101)

X_grid, Y_grid = np.meshgrid(X, Y)
Z = np.exp(-X_grid**2 - 0.5 * Y_grid**2)

fig = plt.figure()
ax = fig.add_subplot()

mesh = ax.pcolormesh(X, Y, Z, cmap="magma")
cb = fig.colorbar(mesh, aspect=15)

plt.show()



For more plot types, see the matplotlib 

plot types overview and the resources therein.

hierarchy.lvl2: Plotting from data file: body mass–metabolism allometry
Plotting from data file: body mass–metabolism allometry

records.content:
Data source: White et. al., 

The scaling and temperature dependence of vertebrate metabolism

# loading dataset using pandas

df = pd.read_csv("data/allometry.csv", index_col=None)
df.info()



Note: pandas support a wide variety of input file formats. See pandas 

Input/output page for more information.

# compute regression coefficients for the power law

from scipy.stats import linregress
fit = linregress(np.log(df["AvgMass"]), np.log(df["Q10SMR"]))
print(fit.slope, fit.intercept)



# generate best fit curve

Mass_th = np.geomspace(np.min(df["AvgMass"]), np.max(df["AvgMass"]), 100)
Q10_th = np.exp(fit.intercept + fit.slope * np.log(Mass_th))



# Plot the data and fit on log-log scale

fig = plt.figure()
ax = fig.add_subplot()

ax.scatter(df["AvgMass"], df["Q10SMR"], marker=".", c="tab:blue")
ax.plot(Mass_th, Q10_th, lw=2, ls="--", c="tab:orange")

ax.set_title("Body mass–metabolism allometry")
ax.set_xlabel("Body mass (g)")
ax.set_ylabel("SMR (mL O$_2$ h$^{-1}$)")

ax.set_xscale("log", base=10)
ax.set_yscale("log", base=10)

# save the figure to image file
#fig.savefig("allometry.png")

plt.show(fig)

In the python scientific computing ecosystem, linear algebra utilities can be found in:

The main numpy package

The numpy.linalg subpackage

The scipy.linalg subpackage

import numpy as np
import numpy.linalg as nla
import scipy.linalg as sla



In the case of numpy, the main documentation for linear algebra operation can be found on numpy’s 

linear algebra page.

Let’s also import matplotlib for plotting purposes

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

From a numpy perspective, a matrix is just a nested numpy array for which dimensions align. For example:

A = np.array([[1, 4], [2, 3]])
print(A)



In principle there is no reason to stop at 2 indices, e.g.,

M = np.array([[[1,2,3], [2,3,4]],[[-1, 0, 1], [-2, 0, 2]]])
print(M)



Two useful ways to build matrices are (1) start with 1D array and reshape it into 2 (or higher) dimensions; (2) use built-in matrix-creating functions

B = np.arange(12).reshape(3, 4)
print(B)



C = np.diag([1, 3, 5, 7])
print(C)



Part of the matrix can be modified via indexed assignment

C[2][1] = -1
print(C)



(Again, consult numpy’s 

guide on array creation for more tips and tricks on array creation.)

Element-wise arithmetics on arrays of matching dimensions

A = np.array([[1, 4], [2, 3]])
B = np.diag([1, -1])



A + B



A * B



Element-wise Arithmetics between arrays and scalars

A ** 2



B * 2



Note: Actually, sometimes element-wise arithmetic can be performed between two numpy arrays of different shapes. The detailed rules is somewhat complicated and is covered on the 

array broadcasting page of the numpy documentation.

A = np.array([[1, 4], [2, 3]])
B = np.diag([1, -1])
X = np.array([-2, 3])



# applying a matrix on a (column) vector 
A @ X



# matrix multiplication
A @ B



Note: For more general “tensor-multiplication” functionalities, consult the 

documentation of the np.tensordot() function.

There are four functions associated with matrix eigensystem:

nla.eigvals() solves the eigenvalues of a general matrix

nla.eigvalsh() solves the eigenvalues of a symmetric matrix

nla.eig() solves both eigenvalues and eigenvectors of a general matrix

nla.eigh() solves the eigenvalues and eigenvecto of a symmetric matrix

# eigensystem of a matrix
A = np.array([[1, 4], [2, 3]])
D, V = nla.eig(A)



print(D)
print(V)



V @ np.diag(D) @ nla.inv(V)



A @ V[:, 1]



D[1] * V[:, 1]

The Leslie matrix describes how population structure changes with time. In one formulation, the matrix takes the general form:\begin{bmatrix}
P_1 & F_1 & F_2 & \cdots & F_{N-1} \\
G_1 & P_2 & 0   & \cdots & 0 \\
0   & G_2 & P_3   & \cdots & 0 \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & & \vdots \\
0 & \cdots & 0 & G_{N-1} & P_N
\end{bmatrix}

Given a population structure (counts of individual per age group) \mathbf{n}_k = [n_{1,k}, n_{2,k}, \cdots, n_{N,k}]^T at time step t(k), the population structure at time step t(k+1) is given by \mathbf{n}_{k+1} = L \, @ \, \mathbf{n}_k

For concreteness, we will use the sea turtles data from 

Crouse et.al., Ecology, 68(5), 1987, where, in the span of a year,

\mathbf{P} = [0, 0.7370, 0.6610, 0.6907, 0, 0, 0.8089]

\mathbf{G} = [0.6757, 0.0486, 0.0147, 0.0518, 0.8091, 0.8091]

\mathbf{F} = [0, 0, 0, 0, 127, 4, 80]

Where the stages represents:

stages = ["eggs, hatchlings", "small juveniles", "large juveniles", "subadults", 
          "novice breeders", "1st-yr remigrants", "mature breeders"]



# Function to build a Leslie matrix given per-age-class birthrate and per-age-class mortality

def make_Leslie_Matrix(F, P, G):
    M = np.diag(P) + np.diag(G, -1)
    M[0, 1:] = F
    return M



F = [0, 0, 0, 127, 4, 80]
P = [0, 0.7370, 0.6610, 0.6907, 0, 0, 0.8089]
G = [0.6757, 0.0486, 0.0147, 0.0518, 0.8091, 0.8091]

L = make_Leslie_Matrix(F, P, G)
print(L)



Exercise 1: Consider a population starting with 5 novice breeder (stage 5) per area, track the number of individuals per area in the first 10 years

Exercise 2: Find the eigenvalues of the sea turtles Leslie matrix. Determine if, in the long term, the population would be growing, declining, or remain stable.

Mathematical background: Let \lambda_1 be the largest eigenvalue of the Leslie matrix L. Then:

|\lambda_1| > 1 implies a growing population

|\lambda_1| < 1 implies a decling population

|\lambda_1| = 1 implies a stable population

To solve ordinary differential equations, we will make use of the integrate subpackage of the scipy package

import scipy.integrate as sint



The main function we will use is the solve_ivp() function from this subpackage, 

documented here.

Let’s also import numpy and matplotlib

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

hierarchy.lvl2: The simplest case: first-order differential equation
The simplest case: first-order differential equation

records.content:
scipy.integrate is capable of solving first-order differential equation of the form \dot{y} = f(t, y), when an initial condition y_0 = y(t_0) is given. As an explicit example, consider the logistic differential equation:\frac{dP}{dt} = r P \left( 1 - \frac{P}{K} \right)

# define the RHS of the differential equation

def f(t, P):
    return r * P * (1 - P / K)

r = 2
K = 100



# use solve_ivp() to solve the differential equation

t_span = [0, 10]
y0 = [20]
out = sint.solve_ivp(f, t_span, y0)

out



plt.plot(out.t, out.y[0])
plt.ylim(0, 105)
plt.show()



# specify evaluation points

t_array = np.linspace(0, 10, 100)

out2 = sint.solve_ivp(f, t_span, y0, t_eval=t_array)
out2



plt.plot(out2.t, out2.y[0])
plt.ylim(0, 105)
plt.show()



# request dense output

out3 = sint.solve_ivp(f, t_span, y0, dense_output=True)
out3



t_array = np.linspace(0, 10, 100)
P_array = out3.sol(t_array)[0]

plt.plot(t_array, P_array)
plt.ylim(0, 105)
plt.show()

scipy.integrate is also capable of solving a system of first-order differential equations. Conceptually, all we are doing is to promote y (and hence \dot{y}) from a scalar to a 1D array. As before, we need to put the system of equation into a standard form \dot{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{f}(t, \mathbf{y}). When \mathbf{y} is 2-dimensional, this expands to:\begin{align}
    \dot{y}_1 & = f_1(t, y_1, y_2) \\
    \dot{y}_2 & = f_2(t, y_1, y_2)
\end{align}

As an explicit example, consider the Lotka-Volterra Predator-Prey Model. The system is (with x being prey and y being predator):\begin{align}
    \frac{dx}{dt} & = \alpha x - \beta x y \\
    \frac{dy}{dt} & = -\gamma y + \delta x y
\end{align}

def predator_prey(t, Y):
    return [
        alpha * Y[0] - beta * Y[0] * Y[1],
        -gamma * Y[1] + delta * Y[0] * Y[1]
    ]

alpha = 24
beta = 0.4
gamma = 2.0
delta = 0.002



t_span = [0, 5]
Y0 = [3000, 50]

out = sint.solve_ivp(predator_prey, t_span, Y0, dense_output=True)



t_array = np.linspace(0, 5, 200)
Y_array = out.sol(t_array)

fig = plt.figure()
ax = fig.add_subplot()

ax.plot(t_array, Y_array[0], c="tab:blue", ls="-", label="prey")
ax.plot(t_array, Y_array[1], c="tab:orange", ls="--", label="predator")

plt.show(fig)



fig = plt.figure()
ax = fig.add_subplot()

ax.plot(Y_array[0], Y_array[1], c="tab:blue", ls="-", label="prey")

plt.show(fig)



# switch to an implicit solver
out2 = sint.solve_ivp(predator_prey, t_span, Y0, method="Radau", dense_output=True)



t_array = np.linspace(0, 5, 200)
Z_array = out2.sol(t_array)

fig = plt.figure()
ax = fig.add_subplot()

ax.plot(Z_array[0], Z_array[1], c="tab:blue", ls="-", label="prey")

plt.show(fig)

hierarchy.lvl2: Cumulative exercise: van der Pol oscillator
Cumulative exercise: van der Pol oscillator

records.content:
The van der Pol oscillator has been used to model cardiac activities. It is a second-order ODE of the form:\frac{d^{2}x}{dt^{2}} - \mu (1-x^{2}) \frac{dx}{dt} + x = 0

For our purpose set \mu = 1.5. Solve the ODE for a variety of initial condition \left(x(0), \dot{x}(0)\right), and plot their phase-space trajectories (x(t), \dot{x}(t)) on the same figure

Hint: scipy.integrate does not directly come with a higher-order ODE solver. However, there is a standard trick that convert a higher-order ODE into a system of first-order ODE. At second order the trick amounts to setting y = \dot{x}, which in itself becomes one of the equations of the system, while the original differential equation becomes an equation of the form \dot{y} = f(x, y, t).

The scipy package provides extensive support for various classical numerical methods. For more details, see the 

scipy user guide.

Interactive widgets rely on the ipywidgets package. In addition, we need to switch the matplotlib backend from inline to ipympl:

import ipywidgets as widgets
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import ipympl



%matplotlib ipympl



As for animation, the functionality is built-in to matplotlib, in the animation module. In addition, let’s import jupyter notebook’s HTML display capabilities:

from matplotlib import animation
import IPython.display as IDisplay



Also import some familiar packages

import numpy as np
import scipy.integrate as sint

Let’s say we want students to explore how initial value affect the prey-predator dynamics. The basic idea is to separate the code into a “setup” part (runs once) and a “callback” part (runs everytime the user interact with the widgets)

# setup code

def predator_prey(t, Y):
    return [
        alpha * Y[0] - beta * Y[0] * Y[1],
        -gamma * Y[1] + delta * Y[0] * Y[1]
    ]

alpha = 24
beta = 0.4
gamma = 2.0
delta = 0.002

t_span = [0, 5]
t_array = np.linspace(0, 5, 400)



# Simple widgets via widgets.interact()

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(10, 4))
ax1 = fig.add_subplot(2, 2, 1)
ax2 = fig.add_subplot(2, 2, 3)
ax3 = fig.add_subplot(2, 2, (2, 4))

plt.show(fig)

def eval_prey_predator(prey_init, pred_init):

    out = sint.solve_ivp(
        predator_prey, t_span, [prey_init, pred_init], 
        t_eval=t_array, method="Radau"
    )
    
    Y_array = out.y

    ax1.cla()
    ax1.plot(t_array, Y_array[0], c="tab:blue", ls="-", label="prey")
    ax1.set_ylim(0, 15000)
    
    ax2.cla()
    ax2.plot(t_array, Y_array[1], c="tab:orange", ls="--", label="predator")
    ax2.set_ylim(0, 200)

    ax3.cla()
    ax3.scatter([prey_init], [pred_init], c="black", s=10)
    ax3.plot(Y_array[0], Y_array[1], c="tab:purple")
    ax3.set_xlim(0, 15000)
    ax3.set_ylim(0, 200)

widgets.interact(
    eval_prey_predator,
    prey_init = widgets.IntSlider(
        min=100, max=10000, step=100, value=3000, 
        description="initial prey density", style={'description_width': 'initial'}
    ),
    pred_init = widgets.IntSlider(
        min=10, max=100, step=1, value=30, 
        description="initial predator density", style={'description_width': 'initial'}
    )
);



# even more control: VBox(), HBox(), Output(), and interactive_output()
# Also: replacing data as opposed to re-initiating plots

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(10, 4))
ax1 = fig.add_subplot(2, 2, 1)
ax2 = fig.add_subplot(2, 2, 3)
ax3 = fig.add_subplot(2, 2, (2, 4))

lines1 = ax1.plot(t_array, np.zeros_like(t_array), c="tab:blue", ls="-", label="prey")
ax1.set_ylim(0, 15000)

lines2 = ax2.plot(t_array, np.zeros_like(t_array), c="tab:orange", ls="--", label="predator")
ax2.set_ylim(0, 200)

sc = ax3.scatter([0], [0], c="black", s=10)
lines3 = ax3.plot([0], [0], c="tab:purple")
ax3.set_xlim(0, 15000)
ax3.set_ylim(0, 200)

prey_widget = widgets.IntSlider(description="prey", min=100, max=10000, step=100, value=3000)
pred_widget = widgets.IntSlider(description="predator", min=10, max=100, step=1, value=30)
output = widgets.Output(layout={'border': '1px solid black'})

with output:
    plt.show(fig)

inputs = widgets.HBox([prey_widget, pred_widget])
ui = widgets.VBox([output, inputs])

def eval_prey_predator(prey_init, pred_init):

    out = sint.solve_ivp(
        predator_prey, t_span, [prey_init, pred_init], 
        t_eval=t_array, method="Radau"
    )
    
    Y_array = out.y

    lines1[0].set_data(t_array, Y_array[0])
    lines2[0].set_data(t_array, Y_array[1])
    lines3[0].set_data(Y_array[0], Y_array[1])
    sc.set_offsets([[prey_init, pred_init]])

widgets.interactive_output(
    eval_prey_predator,
    {"prey_init": prey_widget, "pred_init": pred_widget}
)

display(ui)



For a list of widgets and their interfaces, consult the 

widget list from ipywidgets.

The basic code structure of an animation is similar to the code structure of an interactive: codes need to be separated into an setup part (runs once) and a per-frame part (runs every frame)

# Function to build a Leslie matrix given per-age-class birthrate and per-age-class mortality

def make_Leslie_Matrix(F, P, G):
    M = np.diag(P) + np.diag(G, -1)
    M[0, 1:] = F
    return M



F = [0, 0, 0, 127, 4, 80]
P = [0, 0.7370, 0.6610, 0.6907, 0, 0, 0.8089]
G = [0.6757, 0.0486, 0.0147, 0.0518, 0.8091, 0.8091]

L = make_Leslie_Matrix(F, P, G) / 0.95



N_now = np.array([0, 0, 0, 0, 5, 0, 0])

fig = plt.figure()
ax = fig.add_subplot()
ax.set_ylim(0, 1000)
#ax.set_ylim(1, 1000)
#ax.set_yscale("log", base=10)
ax.set_xlabel("Stage")
ax.set_ylabel("Individuals per area")
bars = ax.bar(np.arange(1, 8), N_now)

def init():
    ax.set_title(f"Population structure at year 1")
    for bar, val in zip(bars, N_now):
        bar.set_height(val)
    return bars.patches

def animate(i):
    global N_now
    if i > 0:
        N_now = L @ N_now
    ax.set_title(f"Population structure at year {max(1, i + 1)}")
    for bar, val in zip(bars, N_now):
        bar.set_height(val)
    return bars.patches
    

anim = animation.FuncAnimation(
    fig, animate, init_func = init, frames=40, interval=500, repeat=False
)

# saving animation to mp4
#anim.save('Leslie_animation.mp4', writer = 'ffmpeg', fps=2)

# save a base64 string of html
